title: GPT-2: Inside a Transformer
description: Fly through the GPT-2 architecture and watch a real forward pass — tokens, embeddings, attention, MLPs, and next-token predictions.

narration.text: Welcome to GPT-2. What looks like magic is really a pipeline: text becomes numbers, those numbers flow through a stack of identical blocks, and out comes a prediction for the next word.
First, each token is turned into a vector — its embedding — and we add a position vector so the model knows word order. This is the start of the residual stream.
Let's feed in a real sentence and run the actual model. Watch the activations come alive.
Here is the first transformer block. Every block does the same two things: attention, then a small neural network called an MLP.
Attention lets each token look at the others and pull in the information it needs. These arcs show how strongly each token attends to the ones before it.
GPT-2 has twelve attention heads per block, each learning a different relationship. Let's isolate a single head.
After attention, the MLP processes each token independently, transforming what attention gathered. Together they update the residual stream.
We can read the actual numbers. Here are the residual activation magnitudes for each token at this layer — down to two decimal places.
These blocks repeat twelve times. As we go deeper, the representations become more abstract — from surface word features to meaning and grammar.
Finally the last vector is unembedded back into vocabulary space, giving a probability for every possible next token. Here are GPT-2's top guesses.
That's the whole machine. Now it's yours — ask me to zoom into any block, isolate a head, show activation values, or run the model on your own text.

affordances.description: Move the camera to frame a part of the model.
Highlight and focus an attention head.
Draw token-to-token attention arcs for a block; omit head to average.
Heatmap residual activations; mode 'values' prints numbers.
Set decimal places for numeric values.
Run the real model on input text.
